evals.prompt: My monthly churn is 9% and it's killing my LTV math. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Starts by separating voluntary from involuntary. If involuntary is more than 35% of total, recommends fixing dunning first as the higher-leverage win. Should pull the split via modern.subscriptions.churn_rate with split=voluntary/involuntary and walk through the diagnostic priority order before recommending tactics.
assertions: Separates voluntary from involuntary churn before any tactic
Calls modern.subscriptions.churn_rate with the voluntary/involuntary split
Names the diagnostic priority order (involuntary first if >35%)
Cites the 20-40% churn reduction available from dunning work
Asks for the split if MCP not connected
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our failed-payment recovery rate is 40%. Is that bad?
evals.expected_output: Diagnoses 40% as below the achievable band. Should walk through the dunning playbook: smart retry schedule (day 0, 2, 5, 9, 14, 21), payment-update prompts with tokenized magic links, pre-expiry card update reminders, backup payment method for high-value subscribers. Should recommend day-14 retry as the highest-marginal-leverage change.
assertions: Identifies 40% as below the achievable band
Recommends the six-retry schedule explicitly
Recommends tokenized magic-link payment update flow
Recommends pre-expiry card update reminders as preventative dunning
Calls modern.subscriptions.dunning_recovery to baseline
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: What should my cancellation flow look like? Right now it's just a confirm-cancel button.
evals.expected_output: Walks through the save flow design framework: cancel reason capture, reason-routed offers, honest exit path. Should describe single-step deflection options (pause, skip, change cadence) and recommend they appear before any retention discount offer. Should warn against burying the cancel button.
assertions: Recommends three-stage flow: reason capture, matched offer, final confirm
Lists single-step deflection options (pause, skip, change cadence)
Recommends these before any retention discount
Warns against burying the cancel button (dark pattern penalty)
Notes the 25-45% deflection rate from one-click options
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Most of my cancellations cite 'too expensive.' Should I offer a discount?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the cancel-reason taxonomy. 'Too expensive' should be answered with pause or downgrade, not discount. Discount erodes the price floor and trains future customers to cancel for a save offer. Should distinguish customers actually citing competitor price (where discount is appropriate) from customers citing absolute price (where it isn't).
assertions: Recommends pause or downgrade over discount for 'too expensive'
Explains why discount erodes the price floor
Distinguishes 'too expensive' from 'found cheaper alternative'
Calls modern.subscriptions.cancel_reasons to confirm distribution
Recommends asking which alternative when customers cite competitors
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: My survival curve looks like a cliff at month 1 and then flattens out.
evals.expected_output: Applies the tenure-curve framework. Month-1 cliff indicates onboarding failure. The intervention is a day-21 check-in with activation prompt, before the month-1 billing event. Should ask about current onboarding sequence and recommend specific interventions before the month-1 renewal moment.
assertions: Identifies month-1 cliff as onboarding failure shape
Recommends day-21 intervention before the month-1 billing event
Asks about current onboarding sequence
Distinguishes month-1 cliff from steady-state churn
Calls modern.retention.cohort_survival to verify shape
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should I make pause and skip more prominent than cancel?
evals.expected_output: Yes, applies the skip-vs-cancel framework. Skip-first architecture deflects 30-50% of cancel intents with no offer cost. Should describe the choice architecture: skip as single-tap, cancel requiring confirmations, skip framed as the considerate option. Should also recommend time-bounded skips over indefinite skips because of the built-in re-engagement moment.
assertions: Recommends skip-first architecture
Cites 30-50% deflection rate
Recommends single-tap skip vs confirmation-gated cancel
Recommends time-bounded skips over indefinite skips
Notes the return-rate difference between skip (60-80%) and cancel (15-25%)
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We have annual subscribers and we want to renew them. What's the best email?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the annual renewal moment intervention. 30 days pre-renewal: value summary email showing what the customer received over the year. Should explain this outperforms a discount renewal offer because it triggers value-recognition rather than cost-evaluation.
assertions: Recommends 30-day pre-renewal value summary email
Explains why it outperforms discount renewal
Names the value-recognition vs cost-evaluation framing
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: How do I know if my voluntary churn is high or my involuntary churn is high?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the diagnostic signals for each. Involuntary signals: high failed-payment count, recovery under 50%, stale card-on-file population, single-payment-method subscribers. Voluntary signals: active cancellation flow firing, cancel reason distribution, survival curve inflections at usage moments rather than billing events. Should recommend tracking them separately if not already.
assertions: Lists involuntary signals (failed-payment count, recovery, card staleness)
Lists voluntary signals (cancel flow firing, reasons, curve inflections)
Notes the recommendation to track separately if not already
Calls modern.subscriptions.churn_rate with the split parameter
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
